###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-08-25T16:24:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgj73v$1t4v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <uOWdnbk6Baccp7bcRVn-qw@giganews.com> <7ugmi0p0h3r77ukbgki9i08k4k3ioe6pbu@4ax.com> <cgfr70$2lks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <m0vmi0p1e99g7sje29tmnt48rie677dlg3@4ax.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no...

> I could be real *evil* and suggest that if you think OO is lacking in
> this area make a submission to J4 - Chuck is *already* groaning and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> from RW - get ready say, "No!"). :-)

Nahhh.  I don't pre-warn the other J4 members on such matters.  I *always*
recommend submitting suggestions for improvements to the standard to J4.

I believe the other members of J4 to be competent to decide for themselves
whether the changes *anybody* suggests have sufficient merit to warrant a
delay in the production of the TR (and the concomitant delay in the
production of the next standard).

When such subjects come up, I might state my opinion as to their viability,
as well as whether they represent a "missing piece" or an "additional
feature", but only in meetings or if correspondence among the J4 members
arises from elsewhere on the committee.  I for one welcome serious and
careful submissions, though I admit being annoyed by frivolous ones.

Many features of 2008 COBOL were designed to be introduced in "minimalist"
form, to be enhanced -- based on real-world experience -- in future
standards if there's enough interest from users, vendors and implementors.
If nobody actually uses a mechanism in practice, it's not clear that adding
a whizbang feature to that mechanism will be worth the effort of either J4
to design it or implementors to provide it!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<cgj73v$1t4v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <uOWdnbk6Baccp7bcRVn-qw@giganews.com> <7ugmi0p0h3r77ukbgki9i08k4k3ioe6pbu@4ax.com> <cgfr70$2lks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <m0vmi0p1e99g7sje29tmnt48rie677dlg3@4ax.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <cgj73v$1t4v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>Many features of 2008 COBOL were designed to be introduced in "minimalist"
>form, to be enhanced -- based on real-world experience -- in future
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can understand and agree with that. With the flexibility of OO 
probably (???) even the proposed minimalist classes for collections 
would suffice say for something like Simulation. On the other hand, 
along comes a whiz bang mathematician who sees a crying need for 
enhancements. Well if there's only one user - diplomatic reply, "Thank 
you for your interest re Simulation. Why don't you take a look at 
FORTRAN ?". :-)

Just don't go too 'minimalist' on me on Collections - and with due 
humility I will provide some 'real world experience' to support my case..

That problem with object references for Collection elements - got it 
licked. I was close, but couldn't get it to work. Fortunately David at 
Newbury spotted it immediately he saw my code As it stands for 
instantiations currently,  in Net Express V 3.1. - not sure about V 
4.0,  you *must* specify global OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION instead of 
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION (2002 Standard). Got into the habit of using 
WORKING-STORAGE thinking of future compatibility - and if you are only 
actually producing ONE instance sometimes it appears to work, others 
not. I've spotted it and changed "WORKING" back to "OBJECT". Ahhh but 
many instances of the same class - then you have got to use 
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION because that's the trigger to the compiler. Mind 
you wouldn't have hurt if the author  who  kick-started the thing had 
provided a quick answer. With a mental blockage on the feature until I 
got it resolved, it certainly has delayed my being able to comment by 
some two weeks..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
On 26 Aug 2004 05:37:57 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>by those of a lower socioeconomic bracket or in a rural area...

I grew up in the lower socioeconomic class. Rather than finding
vibrancy and 'joy of life', I found it pathetic .. an example of how
NOT to live one's life.

>... but maybe it is not this at all, I could be wrong since I'se jes' a 
>COBOL-codin' fool.  

Some would say that's redundant.

>In any case, Mr Wagner, that *you* see no sense in it 
>does not mean that there is no sense to be found in it; to conclude that 
>'I don't understand so it must be without sense' is, in my experience, the 
>mark of an inferior intellect and a base, churlish soul.

I never understood Monty Python, either. A bunch of gay guys acting
like buffos is supposed to be high-art funny? What I find amusing is
intelligent wit such as HL Mencken and this:

Lettered gentility .. is achieved at King's College at the cost of
certain theatricality. It is hard, at times, to recall that the
pictures are real, the port is real, that the candles in the
candlesticks are real candles and not electric lights pretending to be
candles; above all, that the people are creatures of flesh and bone.
In my two years there as a Fellow, I found that a sense of illusion
was rapidly becoming my standard experiential mode. The air of charade
was all-pervasive: the sons of suburbia, like myself, parodied the
lettered gentry; and the lettered gentry parodied the sons of suburbia
parodying themselves ... Brilliant young scientists who knew nothing
of life or art gave ham performances of the role "brilliant young
scientist who knows nothing of life or art." Vain art historians gave
virtuoso performances of "art historian being distastefully vain." And
the institution itself rose repeatedly to heights of dramatic
absurdity.

--Liam Nelson, The Cult of the Fact
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-08-27T03:50:08+00:00

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com> <cgkb1l$csp$1@panix5.panix.com> <0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com> <cglhir$e8a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Don't know about their sexual preferences - but I agree I didn't find 
them funny either. In slapstick mode I did somewhat enjoy John Cleese in 
'Fawlty Towers', occasionally. Same goes for the David Frost group 
TWTWTW (That Was The Week That Was) a bunch of smart-ass ex-university 
types from Oxbridge showing us how terribly clever they were. Of course 
David F, put on a jowl of a look, somewhat like Nixon, and started a 
reasonable career interviewing, for what, a couple of decades. Still 
does the odd interview I believe.

It might have been some of the same, but there was a group early fifties 
that did a stage show. Remember the 'Uncle Tom's Cabin, Siamese ballet 
in R & H's "King and I" There's a chorus line in the original "Poor 
little Eliza", referring to the bastard daughter ?.

They really spoofed that one, complete accentuated Siamese hand 
movements and hopping across the stage. "Poor Little Eliza" became "Poor 
little basket" (bastard)..

But recently returned from RAF in Egypt in '54, I was 'starved'  for 
some sort of culture. For about three months simmersed myself in theatre 
in London, waiting patiently on a Saturday night at ticket offices until 
there were cancellations and then getting in at half price.

"Love of Four Colonels". Written, produced, and acted by Peter Ustinov 
playing the Devil. He enticed four Allied  Lt. Cols. in Berlin to seduce 
the heroine played by an attractive blonde Australian actress, Moira 
Lister. Each colonel picked his own environment, the Russkie - Chekhov, 
the Brit -  Tudor, the Frenchman - Louis XIV and the Yank - a 'thirties 
speak easy. Incredibly funny and absolutely brilliant Ustinov of course.

Shame you probably never had it in the States. I know Archie Bunker 
became an icon, but its British model, "Till Death Do Us Part" or am I 
using the American title ? The lead was played by British/Jewish actor 
Warren ???, mum probably only had about four lines in any one session. 
Would gaze up from her knitting, and right in the middle of a heated 
topic, would utter something tototally iirrelevant like, "Oh look. The 
weather's changing". The son-in-law had one of those bubble cuts of the 
sixties and Alf referred to him as Shirley (Temple). Yes he was a 
blue-collar conservative from Dockland, and as I said Jewish, would make 
utterances like "Yeah then there's your bleedin' 'Arold Wilson. Look all 
them bleedin'  Jews, catholics. Not only that, then again there's all 
those bleedin' blacks taking over the Queen's realm". In mid-sentence 
Alf is interrupted by a knock at the door, still cursing, throws it open 
to be confronted by a smiling Negro parson collecting for some charity !.

Jimmy, Calgary AB.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no...

> Don't know about their sexual preferences - but I agree I didn't find
> them funny either. In slapstick mode I did somewhat enjoy John Cleese in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> does the odd interview I believe.

Although I admit I did enjoy the irreverence of Monty Python, I preferred
the rather more urbane sort of British humo(u)r reflected in (in reverse
chronological order) Beyond the Fringe, Flanders & Swann, and the Hoffnung
Music Festivals.  I'd count Anna Russell in there too, except that I believe
she was an Australian phenomenon.  In my youth, even Victor Borge was often
too broad for my taste.

As to John Cleese and Fawlty Towers:  During my childhood, my home was a
26-room ex-stagecoach-stop hotel that my parents owned and ran in a small
town in Texas, and a *great deal* of the humor in that program hit *very*
close to home.  I believe I have the entire series on tape somewhere.   My
mother was nowhere near social enough for the "oh, I know..." telephone
conversations, but the antics of the "help" in particular were spot-on.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Still, Monty Python has gotten into the psyche.

Some ornithological group in Australia had a long-standing reward for a
specimen of the Australian Night Parrot (whose last sighting was in, like,
1927).

A few years ago, some birders stopped alongside the road for a 'nature
call.' There, at the feet of one, was a dead Australian Night Parrot.

They turned the carcass of the dead parrot in for the reward.

The curator (or factotum) of the society offering the reward said: "This is
straight out of the Monty Python 'Dead Parrot' skit!"
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-08-28T19:52:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k3c1j0l88ovm18954a8ns6pparu4rv9ek6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com> <cgkb1l$csp$1@panix5.panix.com> <0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com> <cglhir$e8a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no> <cgnqrs$1vsr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ka2dnapVMc1aD7LcRVn-ig@giganews.com> <k5SdnVupBNF8VLLcRVn-qg@giganews.com> <217e491a.0408280233.37afd52d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Aug 2004 03:33:24 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Russell Styles" <rws0203@comcast.net> wrote 
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>inventing the phrase referring to EMail.

"Use of the term "spam" was adopted as a result of the Monty Python
skit in which our SPAM meat product was featured. In this skit, a
group of Vikings sang a chorus of "spam, spam, spam . . . " in an
increasing crescendo, drowning out other conversation. Hence, the
analogy applied because UCE was drowning out normal discourse on the
Internet."

http://www.spam.com/ci/ci_in.htm

 

"Take Jakob Nielsen, Internet Usability Guru. The smartest person on
the web, or one of the Web's 10 most influential people, according to
the quotes included on his web page. Back before your mother had
email, Jakob Nielsen was a research scientist at Bellcore, a research
lab jointly funded by a consortium of the Baby Bells, where he studied
usability and user interfaces. But not the Internet. 

Jakob Sends Some Email

In 1994, Jakob moved jobs; he left Bellcore to join Sun Microsystems.
Being an important chap, he didn't want anyone to lose touch with him,
so he decided to send out some email to tell people his new contact
details. This is probably something that any of us would do, but few
of us would think as big as Jakob did. 

First, he trawled through all his archived email, and extracted the
recipient address of every single message. He eliminated the obvious
duplicates, and ended up with a list of some 5500 addresses. So he
made a big mailing list with all 5500 addresses on it, and sent a
notice of his change of address to it. Many of those addresses were
individuals. Maybe even most of them. But lots of them were mailing
lists, some of which pointed at thousands of people. The British HCI
mailing list. The announcements list for the ACM's CHI Special
Interest Group. Lists used to announce talks, or software releases.
Anything. And every one of them got a notice of Jakob's new address. 

Of course, mailing lists have people on them in the end, and most of
us are on more than one list. So people started getting lots of copies
of the message. Three, four, ten, twenty times, the identical message
would appear in your mailbox. I got fifteen, I think, but then they
stopped. I think Jakob had been restrained, because it was noticable
that I only got messages with recipient email addresses in the
alphabetical range A-H. Perhaps after H, Jakob realised that people
were beginning to get the message. 

It was a new phenomenon for most of us. The idea that the same message
would be sent out indiscriminately to thousands upon thousands of
email addresses. It was new then; but these days we have a name for
it. 

Yep. Jakob Nielsen invented spam. 

A Response
Anyway, all this happened in early to mid March. I was sitting around
in the pub with some friends one day, and we were discussing this. We
had proposed that your Nielsen Number was a measure of your
connectedness or importance in the HCI community; how many times did
you recieve Jakob's message? We wondered what was the right response.
And of course, April 1 was coming up... 

I logged into a machine in Edinburgh for which I had administrator
privileges, and created a new account. The name I chose was "Craig
Shergold". Craig's name was well known to anyone who clued in on the
Net. Craig had once been dying of cancer and wanted to get into the
Guiness Book of Records by receiving the largest number of Get-Well
cards ever. The rumour had spread like wildfire on the net, and even
though, many years later, Craig was completely recovered, the hospital
was still innundated, every day, with sackloads of cards. The rumour
proved impossible to kill, especially on the Internet, where it had
morphed into various related forms (and is still sometimes seen). 

On April 1, a message was sent from "Craig's" account to the ACM's HCI
announcement list. It said: 

I head that Jakob Neilsen has recently moved jobs. Does anyone have
his new contact details? 
-- Craig Shergold 

Sure enough, I started getting all sorts of responses from folk who
didn't realise they were being had. The list of respondents who
forwarded me Jakob's message again read like the Who's Who of HCI.
And, of course, Jakob was one of them."


http://www.dourish.com/nielsen.html
```

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com> <cgkb1l$csp$1@panix5.panix.com> <0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com> <cglhir$e8a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no> <cgnqrs$1vsr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ka2dnapVMc1aD7LcRVn-ig@giganews.com> <k5SdnVupBNF8VLLcRVn-qg@giganews.com> <217e491a.0408280233.37afd52d@posting.google.com> <GO-dnUmkr-thiancRVn-hg@giganews.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <rws0203@comcast.net> wrote 

> > >         They are credited with inventing the phrase "SPAM", when
> > >  referring to unwanted email that drowns out all other conversation.

>     They used the word "SPAM" to drown out all other conversation
> in the skit.  Drunken Vikings I think.

http://www.cusd.claremont.edu/~mrosenbl/spamfiction.html#mp
 
>     The parallel was too good for someone (I have no idea who) to ignore.

Well, exactly, but Monty Python cannot be credited with applying the
term to EMail.  Nor can they be credited with inventing the word SPAM
(A trademark for tinned Spiced Ham), it is this that the sketch refers
to.

>     "Tie a yellow ribbon".   I stupid song.  Now part of the culture.

I have seen references that indicate that Yellow Ribbons go back to
the American Civil War.  After the war the returning soldiers did not
know if they would be welcome back to the family because many families
took opposite sides. The display of ribbons at the gates or around
trees indicated a welcome.

That is, the song was specifically written about that type of event.

>     "Mother of all ..." .  I still think that the US Army named their latest
> bomb (Massive Ordinance Airburst, or "MOAB"  wasn't it) so that they could
> call it (informally) the "Mother of all Bombs".

I am not sure how that relates to this discussion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!!

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-08-27T15:28:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no> <cgnqrs$1vsr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9FfQZJd9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9FfQZJd9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...

>    "A fish named Wanda" (is that the english title?), "Time Bandits"
> and the "Life of Brian" are masterpieces.

"A Fish Called Wanda" (1988) was written by John Cleese and Charles
Crichton, and the cast included Michael Palin, but it wasn't technically a
"Monty Python" movie.

"Time Bandits" (1981) was written by Terry Gilliam and Michael Palin,
directed by Terry Gilliam, and included Cleese and Palin in the cast, but
again, that's not everybody.

The "official", for-theater-release Monty Python movies seem to be "Monty
Python and the Holy Grail" (1975), "The Life of Brian" (1979) , and "The
Meaning of Life" (1981).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Sean Connery says he was a delightful man to work with on 'Goldfinger', but couldn't speak a word of English. They got around that by telling him to just speak quickly and they dubbed his dialogue in after.

```
How about this. From somebody in the military while I was in Germany. We met in Dï¿½sseldorf. His very old father came over on a holiday, but couldn't speak a word of German. As was the old gent's norm, he bravely took himself off on a walk. Found a pub, went in and said "Bitter". He got one, a bier - because they probably deemed him to be be so polite  :-) 	

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> Don't know about their sexual preferences - but I agree I didn't find 
> them funny either. In slapstick mode I did somewhat enjoy John Cleese in 
> 'Fawlty Towers', occasionally. 

It is probably a generational thing.  My father doesn't much like
Monty Python either. Nor did he like the Rolling Stones.  There was a
complete change in the 60s for most of us.  You either liked Bing
Crosby _or_ Monty Python.

> Same goes for the David Frost group 
> TWTWTW (That Was The Week That Was) a bunch of smart-ass ex-university 
> types from Oxbridge showing us how terribly clever they were. 

The 'David Frost' group was primarily John Cleese and 'The Two
Ronnies', though they were relative unknowns at the time.  Before
Monty Python that lot were a stage 'review' called 'Cambridge Circus'
referring to their University days (which I went to).
```

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com> <cgkb1l$csp$1@panix5.panix.com> <0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com> <cglhir$e8a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no> <217e491a.0408271155.2b1df2e1@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now didn't you put that nicely :-)               I tend to agree, but 
from memory I think there must be some 10 years between your father and 
me. He as a young Tech Officer and myself still a kid - we both remember 
WWII. Send your dad down Memory Lane when you next see him -  Gracie 
Fields, ("The biggest aspidistra in the world...", "Wish me luck as you 
wave me goodbye...) Vera Lynn (now Dame Vera, and still alive in a 
village in Sussex - the Sweetheart of the Forces, and that includes the 
Canadians in UK - the US had Betty Grable, the pin-up girl, only Warren 
could perhaps confirm if they had a singer who was the US Sweetheart). 
Vera "There'll be bluebells over the white cliffs of Dover, tomorrow 
just you wait and see", "We'll meet again, don't know where, Don't know 
when....". I think Barry Manilow recorded last and wasn't even aware the 
young Vera had done it, probably before he was born.

 Comedians Jack Warner, Tommy Trinder ITMA (It's That Man Again) with 
his Mrs. Mop , "Can I do yer now sir ?". Always finished off with an 
abbreviation. The one I distinctly remember NLYSDDTH (Never let your 
sealegs dangle down the hatch !) -  meant nothing, just a catchy 
gimmick. Does he remember Harry Hemsley with his two imaginary kids 
Winnie and Horace. Horace was about two, and spoke in garble. The catch 
line from Harry was "What is Horace saying now Winnie ?". Must confess I 
sometimes feel like that when reading some of the messages in this group.

Well yes Crosby, sort of. But he was very much a crooner with his own 
style "When the Bh Bh Bh Blue of the night meets the gold of the Day". 
Actually my favourite,  Frank, Chairman of the Board - such clear 
diction and a powerful range.

Rolling Stones. I truly hate 'em. Possibly not the first, but part of 
the 'Great Unwashed Brigade' that moved into music. And old Rubber Lips 
up front doing his thing - Ugh !

Now for singers currently - Shania, Maria Carey, Faith Hill (yummy), J 
Lo, Christine Aguila until she got into the face jewellery scene, 
Brittney, even Jessica Simpson, Beyonce (I ain't got no colour 
prejudice)  - but so many of them are ditz y - but you wont see me 
rushing to switch channels while they are on, strutting their stuff.

>>Same goes for the David Frost group 
>>TWTWTW (That Was The Week That Was) a bunch of smart-ass ex-university 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Be damned if I can recall the Ronnies from the Frost show. But 'The Two 
Ronnies", absolute fun and they were popular over here on PBS (Public 
Broadcasting System) from the States. Our link is Spokane (pronounced 
Spokan) in Washington  State, and two-thirds of their viewers are 
Albertans and a small smattering near the border from B.C. . Just 
remembered Spokane was Bing's hometown. Went there once, smallish and 
quite pleasant. Historically shows its missionary influence, probably 
around 1880-ish, two hospitals called 'Sacred Heart' and 'The Holy Family'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
LX-i wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry about the typo. Thinking of a particular English slang word - I 
rather thought she had two nice t's  :-)

Music taste pretty catholic Daniel. From the classics, (not the real 
heavy stuff) right through to latest where the music is memorable. I 
kinda bypass jazz - I like to hear a musical theme - jazz tends to 
divert where a particular musician sax or whatever improvises - you 
can't hear the original theme. Truly music is so much of my life. I'll 
relate to a particular song and I reminisce about the period it was in.

I've never done opera - usually a bit too heavy - but I truly love some 
of the magnificent themes in Madama Butterfly - and to think the Italian 
audience kiboshed it on opening night.

Which reminds me - our friend Michael took up opera as a hobby. How you 
making out ?. Now if you were lucky like me when I was channel surfing 
one bored Saturday afternoon. I came across Aida (the Egyptian story). 
Bloody heavy and normally I wouldn't watch. The  production was German 
and the director was obsessed with  historical accuracy. Filmed outdoors 
I think. But he was  adamant it should convey what we see in those 
Egyptian paintings in the tombs. So men shaved heads, bare chests and a 
white skirt to the ankles. Remember the paintings of the hand-maidens. 
So the chorus girls wore only a smile and a small piece of bandage over 
their 'privates'. Even one of the lead female singers was bare-breasted, 
although when the camera focused on her, she managed to have her 
arm/elbow discreetly obscuring her nipples. Only watched about ten 
minutes - they should have called it, "Aida goes Topless".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!!

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-08-28T20:58:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgr9o0$p29$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <jQTXc.227450$M95.64181@pd7tw1no> <bs%Xc.30587$5s3.9354@fe40.usenetserver.com> <1Y5Yc.247020$J06.143762@pd7tw2no>`

```
In article <1Y5Yc.247020$J06.143762@pd7tw2no>,
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>I 
>kinda bypass jazz - I like to hear a musical theme - jazz tends to 
>divert where a particular musician sax or whatever improvises - you 
>can't hear the original theme.

How interesting... that is what jazz is supposed to be about - at least 
jazz that starts with a melodic theme, or 'head' - in that it is an 
improvisational form where the musician looks not for the notes that were 
written... but for the 'holes' between the notes, in order to hit them.

Have you ever given a listen to the work of Stephane Grappelli?  Yehudi 
Menuhin said he was like a juggler who tossed ten plates into the air and 
recovered them all; you might want to find a recording of some sessions 
they did together covering 'the standards', things like Summertime, 
Embraceable You, I Got Rhythm, Lady Be Good... all pieces with a 'head'.

Funny thing is that even when he is stating the melody you can hear that 
Grappelli is just *itching* to take off... and take off he does.

DD
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:

> Right On. One late night while taking my Mother in law to the train in 
> Pittsburgh, someone played a couple of his *grappelli* records and the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Warren

Answer Me Oh Lord Above ? Remember him. Tell me Warren, I mentioned Vera 
Lynn as the UK 'Forces Sweetheart" during WWII. There has to be an 
American equivalent, but can only think of Betty Grable the pin-up girl. 
The nearest she came to music was she was married to band leader .....?

GI Joe in India ? You weren't there helping Errol Flynn to win the 
friggin' Battle of Burma were you  :-)  Were you special forces or just 
there to ensure the British Indian Army was doing a pukka job. I say old 
chap, you mustah had a decent cup of tea from a shai wallah. (Still 
that's Egypt - perhaps shai is different in Hindi). On the old BBC radio 
"Goon Show", Peter Sellers - he was in India during WWII, used to 
occasionally throw in a few lines of "Oh the cages in Bombay.....". 
Those in the know in the studio audience would roar with laughter. He 
was referring to hookers hung, ready for sale in wicker baskets.

Ah Nat - Mr Mellow - that voice which was like a smooth glass of sherry 
going down your throat.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!!

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-08-29T14:26:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eIlYc.8516$Es2.4736205@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<29dYc.257119$gE.53602@pd7tw3no>`
- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <jQTXc.227450$M95.64181@pd7tw1no> <bs%Xc.30587$5s3.9354@fe40.usenetserver.com> <1Y5Yc.247020$J06.143762@pd7tw2no> <cgr9o0$p29$1@panix5.panix.com> <41315038.8090204@optonline.net> <29dYc.257119$gE.53602@pd7tw3no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Gee, Jimmy, don't go overboard on me.  I saw Calcutta upon, landing, 
flew to Ledo to help set up tents for the rest of the gang who came
by train...more like a week or two. Volunteered to drive a truck to
Kunming to deliver same to the Chinese, flew back over the hump to
Ledo, and traveled as the shotgun on another convoy to reach a station
in China. Though trained to be a central office repairman, our battalion
replaced the Brits who had been there three years or more, and all I did
for the balance of my duty was drive a six by six on sundry and various
assignments. Once back in India we flew to DumDum in Calcutta and stayed
there until our ship for home was ready. My trip over took 40 days and
went by way of Australia, then to Calcutta. On the ride home, the
trip took 30 days, and the sailors on that ship had fewer days on the
high seas than I had.

I did take a tour of Calcutta, got caught in a riot, and spent two weeks
in camp awaiting the trip home which took us passed some big places and
stopped in Manila (out in the bay) then to Seattle before train  to 
Indiana, and home by bus to Gary. Dad picket me up.


While in China, I played the guitar in a combo that played for the 
Officers Club on Sunday afternoons, and at the Hospital on Saturday
nights for the Officers and Nurses.

I remember all the names of performers you mentioned. As to who was
the USA songstress, I truly don't remember.

Oh, and while in Calcutta on the way home, I got to swim in the pool
at the Victoria Monument.

And, an author of books about India became a favorite. "Night runners
of the Bengal" was the first of his books I read. Can't remember his
name now either.

Warren
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

```
Warren Simmons wrote:

<snip> Sounds like you had an interesting sojourn for a young kid from 
the States.

If you can't remember an American Sweetheart - then I guess there wasn't 
one. That's really strange..

> And, an author of books about India became a favorite. "Night runners
> of the Bengal" was the first of his books I read. Can't remember his
> name now either.
>
Nor can I. Was it  John Masterson perhaps. I think he may have authored 
"Bwahani Junction" (not sure of spelling). Around about the end of 
British rule 1947. Movie - Ava Gardner played a half-chat affected by 
the transition.  I'm thinking of another book of his which interested 
me, and it may be the title you quote. Roughly around the time of the 
'Black hole of Calcutta' incident when the Indians revolted - say about 
1850. This book was to do with the Goddess Kali - her followers killed 
people strangling them with a scarf weighted with a coin. Our 'thugs' 
comes from them - they were thugees. Same book you are talking about ?

Jimmy
```

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-08-29T20:03:23+00:00

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

John Masters is IT. Read everything he wrote I think. The last/first was 
about a young boy as a cabin boy going to Calcutta, or elsewhere, and
then the rest covered that boys life. As I recall the Night Runners 
would do as you said to travelers, dig a hole, and bury the poor guy,
and make a hollow stick to leave in the grave so the site would not
be found.  I believe John was an officer in the Brit Army, and later
in life, came to the USA. For me, the series of books were like a study
of India over time, and left me with a high respect for them as a 
people. They and the Chinese seem as a whole to be very bright. I've
sometimes dreamed of taking a trip from Ledo to China again. I follow
the troubles in Burma where I was also based for a time, and have
read the books about the Burma Surgeon. For a hillbilly from Gary,
it was a first class education. Nothing like you long hairs in this
group though.  I remember the red lips of the betel nut chewers, and
brought back a Gurkha Knife as a keepsake.  Gave it to a Viet transplant
who collected swords. Can't think of a word I learned of Bengali or
Chinese.  Lots of misspells here, but that's not my strong suit
anyway.  I remember Ava. and others, but singers have to jump into
my head when I hear the music.

Re-reading this reminded me that towards the end of my stay, my truck 
had no brakes. The service would not replace the master cylinder as all 
of the parts had been sold to the Brits. Other stuff not being returned 
to the states were buried in a hole using dozer.  I got fairly good at
double clutching to slow down, and roll to a stop. Flew the hump a few
times. Once we got lost, and nearly did not make it back.

Warren
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:

>Trying to remember my parents' musical tastes as WWI and WWII was
>their time, the only ones I can think of that might have come close to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I understand what you are saying about Vera's looks, but surprisingly 
she aged well, and looks very pleasing in old age.

Kate Smith - now that's an interesting mention. I'm not anti-American, 
nor is my Scottish buddy here. I think you'll understand if I say, we 
are a little uncomfortable about the US being the world policeman. And 
when George Dubya, or anybody else says "This is a wunnerful country", 
we squirm a little. Now I saw a black and white on PBS,  really can't 
remember the theme but from the war years Kate Smith sang  "God Bless 
America", with great sincerity. I didn't squirm one bit. I mentioned 
this to my friend, who had also watched the show. His reaction, "Funny 
you should mention that. I had exactly the same reaction. The patriotism 
was meaningful and well placed in historic time".

Jo Stafford, abut the same time as Nat King Cole. I remember fondly, 
"See the <something> in old Algiers, ...., you belong to me". The song 
was very meaningful as I was doing 'penance' - 2.5 years in RAF in 
Egypt, confined to the Suez Canal zone, I never got to see Cairo or any 
of Egypt's wonderful historic monuments or art.

Lena Horne -elegant looking lady, mixed race ? I think she was the first 
to challenge the colour-barrier.. Billie Holliday - sure remember the 
name - but songs - nope, can't remember any.

Jimmy
```

```
On Sun, 29 Aug 2004 18:35:14 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
enlightened us:

>SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No need to apologize about feeling "uncomfortable".  As a VietNam war
vet, I to feel the U.S. should not be the world's policeman and I
squirm everytime GWB says anything!

Kate Smith's rendition of "God Bless America" was very heartfelt and
sincere and has stood the test of time.  The late Ray Charles'
rendition of "America The Beautiful" is in the same vein and will
also, IMO, stand the test of time.

>Jo Stafford, abut the same time as Nat King Cole. I remember fondly, 
>"See the <something> in old Algiers, ...., you belong to me". The song 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

About Lena Horne, she was born Lena Mary Calhoun Horne on June 30,
1917, in the New York City borough of Brooklyn. Both sides of her
family claimed a mixture of African-Americans, Native Americans, and
Caucasians.  But she was always treated as an African-American.  She
was one of the first black performers who were allowed to perform at
the famous New York Cotton Club in the 1930's but not visit as a
customer.

I believe Joshepine Baker may have had an earlier foray into breaking
the color barrier in the U.S. before she fled to France which had no
such barriers.  Ms. Baker was performing in vaudeville for some time
and the New York theater and she is 11 years older than Ms. Horne.

Billie Holiday's most famous songs are most likely:

Strange Fruit written by Lewis Allen -- this was her signature song
for a long time;
God Bless the Child -- which she wrote herself.  I belive the R&B
group Blodd, Sweat and Tears did a cover of this in the 60's or early
70's
Lover Man -- another song she wrote herself -- was a big hit in 1944
She had a string of 3 huge hits while she was recording for Decca
records that were T'aint Nobodys Business if I Do, Them There Eyes and
Crazy He Calls Me.

Unfortunately, Billie had a problem that seems to be shared by among
all the really great jazz/blues singers from then and now.  She had a
heroin and alcohol addiction and even spent 8 months in prison in 1947
for possession of heroin.  It all caught up with her in 1959 when she
collapsed from heart and liver disease.  The stress of withdrawals and
the heart disease took her away on July 17, 1959.  

Some of her music can be found on CD yet today.  If you like blues,
give her a listen to hear where a lot of the more modern blues
originated.  There is also a great book about her called "Lady Sings
The Blues".

Regards,

>Jimmy

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-


"It takes only one drink to get me drunk.
The trouble is, I can't remember if it's 
the thirteenth or the fourteenth."
-- George Burns
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <7VcYc.254062$J06.91749@pd7tw2no>,
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>can't hear the original theme.

[snip]

>>Have you ever given a listen to the work of Stephane Grappelli?  Yehudi 
>>Menuhin said he was like a juggler who tossed ten plates into the air and 
>>recovered them all; you might want to find a recording of some sessions 
>>they did together covering 'the standards', things like Summertime, 
>>Embraceable You, I Got Rhythm, Lady Be Good... all pieces with a 'head'.

[snip]

>OK - maybe it's more the Blues perhaps - I dunno. I definitely like 
>Stephane Grappelli, (at least when I heard him on BBC Radio - but of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>little bit of improvisation - great.

Your taste becomes clearer, Mr Gavan... improvising can be thought of as a 
spice added to the dish of music (synaesthesia, anyone?); Mozart was 
well-known for taking a melody and taking off with it... much of his 
piano-work was never transcribed to paper because he would show up for 
concerts without any sheet music and just... *go*, you might want to 
listen to his Theme and Variations K.265 ('Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star') 
while thinking of it as 18th Century jazz.

As with any spicing... some like more, some less.  Stephane Grapelli might 
cause you to smile while Lenny Tristano might drive you from the table to 
seek solace in other fare... de gustibus non desputandum est and yet... 
what a Wonderful World it is that has New Food in it!

DD
```

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no> <7buqi0dt6adb1hnfr7plba7ltf9c6q6ceh@4ax.com> <cgkb1l$csp$1@panix5.panix.com> <0vhri01rque18qvh61ol9omu16qmhv9520@4ax.com> <cglhir$e8a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <QbyXc.224953$J06.87842@pd7tw2no> <217e491a.0408271155.2b1df2e1@posting.google.com> <jQTXc.227450$M95.64181@pd7tw1no> <bs%Xc.30587$5s3.9354@fe40.usenetserver.com> <1Y5Yc.247020$J06.143762@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> rather thought she had two nice t's  :-)

heh - no argument from me on that...

> Music taste pretty catholic Daniel. From the classics, (not the real 
> heavy stuff) right through to latest where the music is memorable. I 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> relate to a particular song and I reminisce about the period it was in.

I agree with you on that.  (Although I like jazz...)  I remember 
"Angelia" by Richard Marx.  I bought _Repeat Offender_ (his second 
album) the day it came out, and picked that, along with "Right Here 
Waiting" and "Children of the Night", as songs I bet I would hear on the 
radio.  (I used to be pretty good at picking that stuff out.)  Anyway, 
there was a hostess at the restaurant where I worked as a salad bar / 
busboy / dishwasher (usually salad bar, as I had some seniority, and it 
was the cushiest of the tasks).  She and I never got together, but until 
I moved from Chattanooga, we were great friends.  I would get her a 
different Kenny G album each year on her birthday (April 23rd), and we 
had a lot of fun doing different things.

"No One Is to Blame" by Howard Jones and "Make It Real" by The Jets - 
songs I used to listen to at night, when I turned my radio to that 
"sinful" pop station...

"Thunder Kiss '65" by White Zombie - came out around my 21st birthday - 
there were many trips to Atlanta and back where _La Sexorcisto:  Devil 
Music Volume 1_ was spinning in my CD player.  (I can still remember my 
best friend and I singing with it - "Nine-teen sixty.  FIIIIIIIIIVe, 
yeah! WAW!")

George Strait, Dwight Yoakam, Garth Brooks - bring back memories of a 
job at Cracker Barrel, where they played country music all the time, and 
on to Godfather's, where I met a shift manager who like both country 
music and me.  That pretty much evaporated when the store closed, 
although we did see each other a few times after that.

"Before I Knew Your Name" off Bob Carlisle's _Butterfly Kisses and 
Bedtime Prayer_ album, and "I Want to Be Just Like You" by Phillips, 
Craig, and Dean - remind me of when my children were born and dedicated. 
  I still don't know how I was able to sing both of them without crying 
- I usually tear up a little now when I hear either of those songs.

(Yes, I did jump from White Zombie to singin' preachers in two 
paragraphs - interesting, eh?)

(Oh wait - it's YOU that reminisces...  ;>  )

> I've never done opera - usually a bit too heavy - but I truly love some 
> of the magnificent themes in Madama Butterfly - and to think the Italian 
> audience kiboshed it on opening night.

I'm not a big fan of opera, though I do like classical music.  The 
complexity of some of that music is just outstanding.  Whereas REM or 
some country acts are content to use a I, IV, and V chord to make songs, 
a lot of classical uses many different chords and instruments.

> Which reminds me - our friend Michael took up opera as a hobby. How you 
> making out ?. Now if you were lucky like me when I was channel surfing 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> minutes - they should have called it, "Aida goes Topless".

heh - kind of like Wilson from _Home Improvement_ (a TV show with Tim 
Allen - don't know if it made the jump up north).  Anyway, Wilson was 
his behind-the-house neighbor, and you never saw his face the throughout 
the entire series.  Most of the time, it was just the fence, but over 
time, it was comical the number of different things they used to block 
his face.  :)

I'm sure his face wasn't as attractive as that lead singer...
```

```
>job at Cracker Barrel, where they played country music all the time

I worked for a truck-stop company that played hardcore country 24/7. I
didn't know there were commercial-free sources, like elevator music.

Academics don't call it Classical, they call it Art Music. I know that

but it doesn't sound like Beethoven. They say Wagner (my namesake)
started the modern style, which is more complex.
```

```
In article <3k93j0th3gl99rjblqtg5j2bhjmop2sgto@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On Sat, 28 Aug 2004 22:14:33 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

>>I'm not a big fan of opera, though I do like classical music. 
>
>Academics don't call it Classical, they call it Art Music. I know that
>because a former gf had a PhD in it.

We must know different academics, Mr Wagner; ones that I know refer to 
'classical music' quite readily when they want to describe music of the 
style dominant between the Baroque and the Romantic.

>People are still composing it,
>but it doesn't sound like Beethoven. They say Wagner (my namesake)
>started the modern style, which is more complex.

After Brahms (whose first symphony has been called 'Beethoven's 10th) the 
kind of musical expressions began to change a bit more rapidly, aye... I 
don't know who 'they' are but Wagner was taught to be, in my time, was 
taught to be firmly in the Romantic camp.  'Modern' style was taught to 
have begun with Schoenberg (for tonality) and Stravinsky (for rhythm)... 
but over the decades curricula can change, sure.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!! [drifting WAY OT]

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-08-30T10:24:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgvnt6$182t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1Y5Yc.247020$J06.143762@pd7tw2no> <uSbYc.31632$5s3.7498@fe40.usenetserver.com> <3k93j0th3gl99rjblqtg5j2bhjmop2sgto@4ax.com> <cgsqsg$3io$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cgsqsg$3io$1@panix5.panix.com...
> 'Modern' style was taught to have begun with Schoenberg (for tonality)

Or, perhaps, *atonality*.  Personally, I think *atonal* is inaccurate; once
you develop an ear for it, dodecaphony is just a different way of
approaching music (I found myself humming the first movement of Webern's
First Cantata for some time after having performed it a few decades back).

While Schoenberg's establishment of the strict rules for dodecaphonic
composition were indeed seminal, I'd also argue that Impressionism (Debussy,
Ravel) and the anti-Romantic, anti-Impressionistic music of Satie and his
followers predated Schoenberg and were also seminal.  Then there's
polytonality, to be found in both Hindemith and Ives, also
pre-Schoenbergian.

I think "Modern" as a term to describe an era of music has lost most of any
meaning it might once have had!

As to early "modern-sounding" pieces from rather earlier, check out
"Prophetiae Sybillarum" of Orlando di Lasso (and, of course, the works of
Don Carlo Gesualdo).

    -Chuck Stevens



    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
them in this particular forum.

(stray thought - 'degree mill' is a term sometimes applied to those buy 
Your Papers Here organisations that have their campuses in a post-office 
box... it tickles my sense of The Perverse to think of using such 
establishments to get diplomae in, say, Music History, Medieaval French 
and Comparative Slavic Literature.)

>
>I think "Modern" as a term to describe an era of music has lost most of any
>meaning it might once have had!

Ahhhhh, for the Oldene Dayse... when Modern could be used to describe such 
things as Post-Neo-Reconstructo-ReactoModern cannot, today!

>
>As to early "modern-sounding" pieces from rather earlier, check out
>"Prophetiae Sybillarum" of Orlando di Lasso (and, of course, the works of
>Don Carlo Gesualdo).

I have been introduced to a bit of their works, aye... years on back I 
remember a fellow putting a record on the turntable which features 
recordings of various musics from the Second Papal Court at Avignon... 
downright avante-garde, they sounded.

DD
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cgsqsg$3io$1@panix5.panix.com...
>  
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Wow. You've mentioned your musical tastes before. We both like classical 
but I think we are on a slightly different wavelength. If I was 
researching and followed your notes above about Debussy and Ravel - I 
don't think I would have ever gone looking ! As it is I love their 
music, both. Perhaps it's because I like, for want of a name, the 
'romanticists'.  Those both with a sense of glory and sadness.

Ravel - as soon as I hear Bolero - I want to outdo George Raft and get 
up and start clicking my heels, flamenco style. Debussy, so much 
nostalgia, 'L'apres midi du Faun', (that's my best crack at French). BBC 
used a theme from Spartacus (Katchachurin - spelling ?), for 'The Onedin 
Line' - a merchant family around mid to late 1800s in Liverpool. TV show 
started with a magnificent shot of a sailing vessel accompanied by the 
Spartacus theme, starting off sweet and low, rapidly increasing pace and 
finishing up with a beautiful crescendo - I believe the BBC show 
'popularised' the Spartacus music. (Although as far back as WWII 
Katchachurin was know for his 'Sabre Dance'). Then again Gustav Holst's 
Planet Suite, Mars the Bringer of War, I think, which has been turned 
into a beautiful hymn, "And I vow to thee, my country....". Elgar and 
his Enigma Variations - I could go on and on...........

Sadly I can't read music nor do I play any instrument.

Can't all be the same. Different strokes for different folks.

And Doc in some message threw in something like "Pilate asked was it 
true.....". No I didn't go searching the Web as he suggested - haven't 
got the time. But I zeroed in immediately what he was referencing. With 
a teacher who was DEDICATED  and had a VOCATION for his topic, I loved 
English - although I must confess I have not read as much literature as 
I should have. But at 14 one of our text books was a set of essays. It 
included I think something from de Quincy - probably admission to being 
an opium eater, an extract from Robert Louis Stevenson ( is that a "v" 
or "ph" ?), 'Travels with a Donkey" - and the very first essay in the 
book - from Francis (?) Bacon with the opening line, "What is Truth, 
said jesting Pilate.....".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> On Sat, 28 Aug 2004 22:14:33 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I like Kenny G but the cognosenti don't. They say he's repititious. 

His solo on Silhouette, I believe, from his live album - man.  :)  If I 
could breathe like that, I'd pass my fitness test with flying colors!

I can't believe Jimmy got me on this reminiscent tangent - I hadn't even 
thought about her for quite a while.  Everyone called her "Angie", and 
that song came out about the time we became friends.  I started calling 
her "Angel", and darned if other folks didn't start picking up on it. 
It was a subtle change, but instead of being Angie the lounge waitress, 
she became Angel the hostess, and last I heard, was the head hostess, 
which is part of the management chain for that restaurant.  That's 
impressive, given that it's family-owned, and management doesn't really 
change much there.

I don't know if the nickname change had anything to do with it, although 
I'd like to think that our friendship helped her feel better about 
herself as person.  Things might have worked out the same even if I had 
never worked there - as DD said in another thread, you never 
knooooo-ooooow!  :)

>>George Strait, Dwight Yoakam, Garth Brooks - bring back memories of a 
>>job at Cracker Barrel, where they played country music all the time
> 
> I worked for a truck-stop company that played hardcore country 24/7. I
> didn't know there were commercial-free sources, like elevator music.

Muzak has lots of different offerings.  The aforementioned restaurant 
ordered big tapes (they looked like oversized 8-tracks), that played 
something like 4 hours straight.  On the weekends, we'd start to hear 
repeats toward the end of the shift.  Godfather's used Muzak too, but 
used their adult contemporary channel.  Cracker Barrel just used the 
local country music station, which was fine - Sunday nights was classic 
night, and they'd play some _really_ nostalgic music.  :)

These days, you can get the same thing on digital cable or XM.

>>I'm not a big fan of opera, though I do like classical music. 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> started the modern style, which is more complex.

Interesting.

That line about your gf reminds me of a joke...  A man and his wife were 
both graduating from college - he with his doctorate, she with her 
masters degree.  They walked in their house after graduation, and the 
man turned to his wife and said, "Honey, from now on, I want you to call 
me 'Doctor'."  She replied, "Okay, as long as you call me master."

(Okay, I didn't say it was that good of a joke...  ;>  )
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol data format!!! urgent!!! [drifting WAY OT]

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-08-31T00:23:49+00:00

```
On Sun, 29 Aug 2004 20:42:40 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>His solo on Silhouette, I believe, from his live album - man.  :)  If I 
>could breathe like that, I'd pass my fitness test with flying colors!

I listen to his "Breathless" album daily.


>> Academics don't call it Classical, they call it Art Music. I know that
>> because a former gf had a PhD in it. People are still composing it,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Interesting.

True.

>That line about your gf reminds me of a joke...  A man and his wife were 
>both graduating from college - he with his doctorate, she with her 
>masters degree.  They walked in their house after graduation, and the 
>man turned to his wife and said, "Honey, from now on, I want you to call 
>me 'Doctor'."  She replied, "Okay, as long as you call me master."

The BDSM folks  think sex is about 'power transfer'. I find it  kinda
pathetic. There's no sex in their 'sex'. Their explanation of why is a
warped legalism that I don't believe.
```

```
"Bush draws line at Britney

....But the right-wing has drawn the line at Britney Spears. When word 
leaked out that White House strategists wanted to give Spears, the 
formerly virginal teenage pop sensation now desperately trying to 
misbehave her way into the rock 'n roll hall of shame, a starring role 
in New York, the flood of protest was biblical in scale.

Although a vocal supporter of Bush and a woman with a celebrity to match 
the Hollywood star power available to Democrats, the idea rapidly proved 
as welcome as having French President Jacques Chirac represent the 
Alabama delegation.

Citing Spear's "55-hour marriage, her lesbian kiss with Madonna (Me - 
the latter - what an absolute Ratbag !) and all-round white trashiness", 
an array of conservative groups e-mailed the convention organizers to 
warn that her presence in New York would be a moderating step too 
far...... etc..."

She wont be attending. Jeepers ! Perhaps George Dubya has got something 
right at last !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<455feb1d.0408232110.50e690ee@posting.google.com> <uOWdnbk6Baccp7bcRVn-qw@giganews.com> <7ugmi0p0h3r77ukbgki9i08k4k3ioe6pbu@4ax.com> <cgfr70$2lks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <m0vmi0p1e99g7sje29tmnt48rie677dlg3@4ax.com> <cgioga$1je6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no...

> "Who be 'we' ? Never realized you had visited my beloved Somerset on
> vacation.

Actually, I think I have, if a town in the Northeast of that region (but I
think administratively separate) counts.

If a little bit of Georgian architecture executed in yellow-tan stone is
good, a WHOLE LOT of Georgian architecture executed in yellow-tan stone has
to be a WHOLE LOT better, right?   Thank you, Messrs. Wood (pere et fils),
but enough is enough ...  ;-)

Unless you were talking about the Somerset that the Boerne Greyhounds
football team used to play against back in the 1950's, which I visited a few
times as a member of the Boerne High School Marching Band ... Nice town, I
guess, but "beloved?"      ;-)

I didn't pick up the usage from either of these locations, by the way, but I
don't know where; most likely, I think is the quote "I doubt me an it be
commercial" from a Flanders & Swann bit about (as I recall) the origins of
Greenfleeves.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
>news:lm8Xc.216394$gE.12574@pd7tw3no...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You pulling my leg. Just to ensure we are on the same wavelength. I'm  
talking county of Somersetshire, bordered by Wiltshire, N.E., 
Dorset(shire) S.E. and Devon(shire) W. Or are you talking about Somerset 
in Kentucky . There, bet you didn't know I knew that. Dead easy; about 5 
years back met a doctor at Lake Louise from Somerset, Ky. Ironically my 
son lives in a new housing development SE of me in Calgary called Somerset.

>If a little bit of Georgian architecture executed in yellow-tan stone is
>good, a WHOLE LOT of Georgian architecture executed in yellow-tan stone has
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Presumably you are talking Bath ? Fascinating place. Peer down to the 
Roman baths (spa) some 30 feet below and you are standing on 30 feet of 
human debris built up over the centuries. Could of course go to Cheddar 
Gorge, interesting caves with stalagmites(upwards) and 
stalactites(downwards) - the way I remember that is tight(tite) "hang 
on". There's  not an ounce of Cheddar made there. Then of course Bristol 
'all shipshape and Bristol fashion', which used to be part of 
Zummerzett. My favourite sherry drink, Harvey's Bristol Cream - recent 
years reverted to selling it in blue bottles. Kind of puts me off 
thinking of methyl hydrate - but in fact the colour is kosher, 'Bristol 
Blue'. Taunton nice county town, no big deal. Ever read R.D. 
Blackmore's, or seen on PBS "Lorna Doone". 'iz book shure makes a reel 
effert to uuse Zummerzet dilict. Location,  lovely Exmoor - very 
occasional stag.

You wouldn't probably have picked up on it but steeped in Arthurian 
legend. As a romanticist, how I so much wish a real person had existed. 
I think the legend is based on an amalgam of historical figures, both 
from southern Wales and the south-western part of England. At long last 
the latest movie, which I haven't seen, sets it in the correct period 
Romano-Britain just as the Romans were slowly retreating back to Italy. 
Close to where I lived some five miles east of Taunton, a nondescript  
hillock in a field Camel(ot). North of me in ancient times, (pre-Roman), 
were the Lake Dwellers, just like in modern Indonesia,  people living in 
houses built on stilts above water level.. Being largely under water the 
area towards Glastonbury Tor ties in with the legend. Supposedly Arthur 
and his queen are buried there. Just slightly north of me, you are 
through the place in about a minute in a car, where Alfred (the Great) 
took shelter with a woodsman and his wife while pondering how he could 
beat the Danes. As the school story goes the wife warned Alfred to watch 
her cakes/scones. Deep in thought about the Danes - "Alfred burnt the 
cakes".

During the monastic period it was the monks who taught the locals to 
build channels or canals to drain the soil - leaving behind superb land 
for grazing cows - making Somerset a haven for dairy herds. Back in the 
middle ages, the sea or an inlet used to go inland to Creech St. Michael 
where I lived, some thirty miles at least from the N. Somerset coast.
.
Some five miles north of Taunton at Western Zoyland I think - the last 
invasion/battle on English soil. The Duke of  Monmouth the bastard son 
of Charles II, landed on the Dorset coast, moved up to Taunton and was 
challenged at W.Z. by the army of James II, (his 'uncle'). Carted back 
to Taunton, the rebels  were quickly tried and hanged on the orders of 
the notorious Judge Jeffreys. Jeffreys figures in 'Lorna Doone'. 
Monmouth was initially taken east over-nighting at Guildford, county 
town of Surrey, and then on to the Tower "where they done 'im in" - 
probably the axe.

The county has a very silent history, like much of UK. You pass through 
some nondescript place and don't realize its connection with the past.

>Unless you were talking about the Somerset that the Boerne Greyhounds
>football team used to play against back in the 1950's, which I visited a few
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No I don't think so. A discordant off-tone School Marching Band in 
England  back in my time - so terribly gauche.

>I didn't pick up the usage from either of these locations, by the way, but I
>don't know where; most likely, I think is the quote "I doubt me an it be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Can't remember but Flanders and Swann, were they the pair who sang "I'm 
a ger-noo (Gnu) "

>    -Chuck Stevens
>
>
>  
>
Serious note. It hit me this morning. Why SortedCollections don't figure 
in the proposal. Using your open mind, follow this one through. I told 
you I got that ListRecord class to work; I sent you a copy. Now take a 
peek at what I sent you. Remember it's an object - so how would you sort 
it. Can be done - but long-winded. "Nah ignore it. Folks can worry about 
sorting later if they must". *I will be covering the point in some detail !*

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
